text:
It was supposed to be a day of national glory for France as the nation welcomed sportspersons and spectators from the world over to the opening ceremony of the Olympics. Instead, well before the ceremony began, the nation made international headline for nationwide sabotage of its railway network.

The sabotage of the railways was just the beginning. Through the day, incidents continued to mar the day’s revelry.

From the train sabotage to rain and ticketing snag, here is all that went wrong on the day one of Paris Olympics.

Train sabotage

The French high-speed rail network was hit by acts of sabotage on Friday, according to operator SNCF.

The operator described the incidents as “malicious acts”.

The attacks included acts of arson that stopped the trains’ functioning and stranded thousands of commuters hours ahead of the Olympics opening ceremony in Paris.

Around 4 am (local time) the arsonists cut and burned cables used for signalling and security, reported The New York Times citing French authorities.

France 24 reported that incidents occurred at three places and the sabotage at a fifth place was foiled. It reported that incidents signal boxes were damaged along the lines connecting Paris with cities of Lille in the north, Bordeaux in the west, and Strasbourg in the east.

The report quoted two security sources as saying that the modus operandi led to suspicions on leftist militants or environmental activists. The sources, however, said that there was no evidence yet for the same.

Rains spoil the opening ceremony

French weather service had predicted that “flooding rains” will occur on Friday and coincide with the Olympics opening ceremony.

The prediction did not prove to be wrong as sporadic rainfall kept spattering the ceremony even as spectators largely remain put to witness what some called once-in-a-lifetime spectacle in Paris. The Associated Press reported that skies above Paris were grey with intermittent drizzle throughout the afternoon but they cleared as the ceremony began but the showers picked up soon just as the procession began advancing the Seine River.

Instead of a traditional march in a stadium, the Paris Olympics opening ceremony featured more than 6,500 participating athletes rowing in boats in the Seine River. The AP reported that mood largely remained lively among the spectators but, as the rain grew heavier, a small trickle of them left the ceremony while the most of the people stayed put and braved the showers.

Ticketing snag and long queues add to spectators’ woes

Thousands of spectators faced long queues and ticketing problems upon their arrival in Paris for the opening ceremony, according to AFP.

The agency reported that many gates opened an hour late and two gates opened at least two hours late because concerned staffers had not been delivered scanners to check tickets.

“The police have been amazing, but the organisation sucks,” said German film producer Michael Ohoven, 48, to the agency.

Ohoven told the agency that he had been waiting an hour in front of a closed gate. The agency reported that another man needed first-aid after he collapsed.

To add to the woes, heavy rain began when the doors opened at last.

Long lines of people were also seen close to the Louvre in central Paris and large crowds also built up close to the start point of the river parade around the Austerlitz bridge, according to the agency.

Separately, The New York Times reported that it was “hard to overstate how daunting” it was for spectators to move around to witness the opening ceremony. The newspaper reported that as subway stations were closed, they were put on buses that did not take them to their destinations at once.

“Itâs hard to overstate how daunting the logistics of this ceremony have been for spectators, who tried to get into subway stations that were closed, were put on buses that didnât go where they were meant to go and crowded en masse at confusing checkpoints before gaining entry to their spots along the route. They had to travel for hours to even get here. Itâs such a shame that the rain sent many of them heading for the exits early,” noted Sarah Lyall of The Times.

Rain-induced departures

Even though spectators braved the initial spells of rains, “a procession of departures” began once the French contingent had passed in the parade, according to The Times.

“A deluge from the skies and a deluge for the exits: The crowds at Pont Neuf have seen enough now that the French team has passed. A procession of departures,” noted Tariq Panja of The Times.

Erika Kirk delivered an emotional speech from her late husband's studio, addressing President Trump directly. She urged people to join a church and keep Charlie Kirk's mission alive, despite technical interruptions. Erika vowed to continue Charlie's campus tours and podcast, promising his mission will not end.

description: Even before the Olympics opening ceremony began, arsonists attacked the French railway network in at least three locations
